coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of multifaceted palladium nanoparticles supported on nitrogen-doped carbon dots for enhanced oxygen reduction and magnetic field response studies
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kakaei, Karim
Aykut, Yasemin
Bayrakçeken, Ayşe
Mahdavi, Mehdi
coredata.dc:description: This study introduces a novel electrode material composed of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) decorated with palladium nanoparticles. The N-CQDs were synthesized using a pyrolysis like method involving glucose and urea and characterized using various techniques, including TEM, FTIR, Raman, SEM, EDS, XPS and XRD. The nitrogen content of the N-CQDs was found to be 20 % by elemental analysis. Multifaceted palladium nanoparticles were subsequently anchored onto the N-CQDs and utilized as a catalyst for enhanced oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in an alkaline medium. A magneto-electrochemical system was established to comprehensively investigate the influence of magnetic fields on the electrocatalytic process. The results demonstrate that the electron transfer number remains relatively constant at approximately 4 for both magnetized and non-magnetized Pd/N-rCQDs while the tafel slope exchange from 36 mV/dec to 40 mV/dec. However, the application of a magnetic field significantly improves the performance in both the mixed and diffusion regions of the ORR, with an enhancement of approximately 100 %. These findings highlight the potential of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots, metal nanoparticles and the synergistic effects of magnetic fields in optimizing electrocatalytic reactions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-Reduced carbon quantum dots
Pd nanoparticles
ORR
Magnetic field

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs, N-CQDs, r-CQDs, N-rCQDs, Pd/N-rCQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work has been supported by the Scientific and Technological Research Council of T\u00FCrkiye (T\u00DCB\u0130TAK 2221 program) (project No. 1059B212400282 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) (i and ii) SEM images (iii) TEM image and histogram of Pd/N-rCQDs, (b) (i) EDS mapping images based on the SEM image and (ii) EDS spectra, and (c) (i, ii) FT-IR spectra of CQDs, rCQDs, N-CQDs, N-rCQDs, and Pd/N-rCQDs catalysts.

Characterization of CQDs, N-CQDs, N-rCQDs, and Pd/N-rCQDs. (a) Raman spectra of CQDs and N-CQDs. (b) Raman spectra of N-rCQDs and Pd/N-rCQDs. (c) XRD pattern of Pd/N-rCQDs befor reaction and after prolong reaction after 2000 cycle (d) XPS survey spectrum of Pd/N-rCQDs. (e) Deconvoluted Pd 3d XPS spectrum. (f) Deconvoluted N 1s XPS spectrum. (g) Deconvoluted C 1s XPS spectrum.

(a) CV of N-rCQDs in the presence of N2 and O2 at 0.1 M KOH. (b) CV of Pd/N-rCQDs in the presence of N2 and O2 at 0.1 M KOH. (c) CV of Pd/N-rCQDs in the presence and absence of magnetic field in O2 saturated at 0.1 M KOH. (d) Nyquist plots measured at 0. 6 V vs RHE from 100 kHz t0 100 mHz in the absence of magnetic field and in presence of different magnetic field. (e) LSV of Pd/N-rCQDs with various RPM in 0.1 M KOH and O2 saturated in absence of magnetic field. (f) Koutecky'-Levich plots of the Pd/N-rCQDs derived from RDE voltammograms in (e) at different potentials. (g) LSV of Pd/N-rCQDs with various RPM in 0.1 M KOH and O2 saturated in presence of magnetic field after 2000 cycles. (h) Koutecky'-Levich plots of the Pd/N-rCQDs derived from RDE voltammograms in (g) at different potentials. (i) The Tafel plot of the ORR on the Pd/N-rCQDs electrode with and without magnetic field in 0.1 M KOH derived from LSVat 1600 rpm (j) Chronoamperometric profiles of Pd/N-rCQDs and Pt/C in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH at 0.6V, showing the effect of 0.2 M methanol addition at 900s.
Summary of bulk, surface compositions, and ICP of the catalysts.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of multifaceted palladium nanoparticles supported on nitrogen-doped carbon dots for enhanced oxygen reduction and magnetic field response studies

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Maragheh, P.O. Box. 55181-83111, Maragheh, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University Of Maragheh, P.O. Box. 55181-83111, Maragheh, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Atatürk University, Erzurum, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Ataturk University, Erzurum, Turkey

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Maragheh, P.O. Box. 55181-83111, Maragheh, Iran.

head.ce:miscellaneous: Handling Editor: Dr M Mahdi Najafpour

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study introduces a novel electrode material composed of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) decorated with palladium nanoparticles. The N-CQDs were synthesized using a pyrolysis like method involving glucose and urea and characterized using various techniques, including TEM, FTIR, Raman, SEM, EDS, XPS and XRD. The nitrogen content of the N-CQDs was found to be 20 % by elemental analysis.

Multifaceted palladium nanoparticles were subsequently anchored onto the N-CQDs and utilized as a catalyst for enhanced oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in an alkaline medium. A magneto-electrochemical system was established to comprehensively investigate the influence of magnetic fields on the electrocatalytic process.

The results demonstrate that the electron transfer number remains relatively constant at approximately 4 for both magnetized and non-magnetized Pd/N-rCQDs while the tafel slope exchange from 36 mV/dec to 40 mV/dec. However, the application of a magnetic field significantly improves the performance in both the mixed and diffusion regions of the ORR, with an enhancement of approximately 100 %.
These findings highlight the potential of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots, metal nanoparticles and the synergistic effects of magnetic fields in optimizing electrocatalytic reactions.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymer electrolyte membrane fuel cells are seen as a new candidate for power supply and are ecofriendly. Researchers are actively developing fuel cells to work at low temperatures; however, there are still significant challenges to overcome. Major problems are related to the electrocatalyst, such as catalyst stability, poisoning resistance, cost of the catalyst, and surface to volume. To address these limitations, scientists are making continuous efforts [1,2].

Carbon allotropes, including multi-walled carbon nanotubes, graphene, fullerene, Vulcan Carbon, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), are among the most important supports in the field of electrochemical energy storage [3–5]. Recently, CQDs have attracted significant interest in the fields of electrochemical devices [6], photocatalytic [7], bioimaging [8], biosensing [9], and drug delivery applications [10].

CQDs play an important and promising role in exploiting energy storage materials, contributing to the advancement of energy storage devices. The properties of CQDs can be tailored by manipulating the interactions between the carbon core and functional groups. CQDs possess numerous active sites, a high specific surface area, high electron conductivity, and a small size, which facilitate ion transfer and storage. The controllable surface structure allows for easy functionalization through covalent and noncovalent bonding, enabling the adjustment of physicochemical properties and the design of new materials with specific desired characteristics [11,12]. Hence, the preparation of highly nitrogen-doped carbon based materials has important scientific significance for ORR in the cathode side of fuel cells [13] and Li ion batteries(LIBs) [14]. Thus, one expects to exploit a way of creating novel highly nitrogen-doped porous carbon materials based on CQDs. For example, VO2 arrays on graphene Cdots, CuO nanowires@ graphene CDots, and MoS2 with CQDs, have been studied as anode for LIBs [15–17]. Pan et al. developed a nitrogen-doped (6 atom %) core-shell carbon nanotube for LIBs. This material exhibited an impressive specific capacity of 390 mAh g−1 at 1.488 A g−1 [18]. Lu et al. reported a porous carbon scaffold with 5.6 atom % nitrogen doping, demonstrating 1275 mAh g−1 at 186 mA g−1 for LIBs [19]. Several studies have investigated the potential and performance of multiheteroatom-doped CQDs as catalysts for the ORR. These studies have explored the relationship between the structural properties of CQDs and their ORR activity [20,21]. For example, Sun and coworkers [22] demonstrated that co-doping graphene quantum dots with fluorine and nitrogen can enhance their electron distribution, improving their electrocatalytic performance for ORR.

Scientists are particularly interested in studying metal nanoparticles, especially those made from noble metals. This is because these nanoparticles have a large surface area and unique properties that are different from bulk metals. These unique properties are mainly influenced by the size, shape, composition, and how ordered the atoms are inside the particle (crystallinity) of the nanoparticles [23]. Montejo-Alvaro et al. utilized density functional theory (DFT) calculations to demonstrate that defective quantum dots enhance the stability of palladium nanoparticles (Pd NPs) and decrease the adsorption energies of reaction intermediates on Pd/defective graphene dot catalysts, thereby improving their ORR performance [24]. Experimental evidence suggests that utilizing nitrogen-doped carbon nanomaterials as catalysts and support materials for Pd NPs can lead to improved dispersion, smaller particle size, and enhanced performance for the ORR [25–27]. Palladium is a compelling alternative to platinum for the ORR in alkaline environments due to its comparable or superior activity, enhanced methanol tolerance, improved stability, and reduced susceptibility to poisoning. This makes Pd-based catalysts promising candidates for the cathodes of alkaline fuel cells [28,29].

Recent studies have demonstrated that magnetite and static magnetic fields can enhance the overall electrocatalytic performance of fuel cells [30]. This emerging field, known as magneto-electrochemistry, offers promising avenues for improving energy conversion devices [31].

The investigation of magnetic field effects on electrochemical reactions, such as HER, OER, and ORR, is a growing area of research interest. These studies offer valuable insights into fundamental electrochemical principles and hold significant promise for improving reaction efficiency and selectivity [32–35]. Magnetic fields are simple to apply, effectiveness in various environments, and negligible energy consumption. This makes it easy to modify electrochemical systems using readily available and cost-effective magnets [36].

When studying the impact of magnetic fields on electrocatalysis, it is crucial to differentiate between kinetic effects and mass transport effects. Kinetic effects influence the reaction rate by altering the reaction mechanism or electron transfer kinetics. Mass transport refers to changes in the transport of species to and from the electrode surface. Additionally, magnetic fields can enhance gas evolution by reducing bubble adhesion (MHD effect) and improve paramagnetic material transport (Kelvin force). Magnetothermal effects and spin state changes also boost water splitting and oxygen reactions [32,37]. For instance, Yan et al. [38] found that a 350 mT magnetic field improved nanocage catalyst OER/ORR by using the Lorentz force to influence electron transfer kinetics, mass transport, and Co spin polarization, enhancing electron transfer and oxygen adsorption [36]. Likewise, Yang et al. [39] achieved a 160 % increase in ORR current density with a mild magnetic field by employing the Lorentz force to boost oxygen diffusion and mass transfer via bulk and surface MHD.

Previous studies have employed non-magnetic electrodes, such as platinum and gold, to isolate the effects of magnetic fields on the electrolyte, independent of electrode kinetics. This approach avoids the confounding influence of inherently active magnetic electrode materials like ferromagnetic catalyst [32]. In contrast, magnetic materials like Pd NPs, which are resistant to oxidation and readily form stable, small nanoparticles with numerous surface spins, could be advantageous for investigating the direct impact of magnetic fields on the catalytic activity of nanocatalysts [40]. Without a magnetic field, the surface spins of Pd are randomly oriented, exhibiting a paramagnetic-like state with no distinct spin-charge organization. However, applying a magnetic field magnetizes the surface Pd atoms, causing their spins to align in a parallel, ferromagnetic-like arrangement that exhibits spin-charge distribution. This transition from paramagnetic to ferromagnetic spin configuration on the Pd nanoparticle surface upon exposure to an external magnetic field is also noted in Refs. [40,41].

In this study, N-CQDs were synthesized using a simple one-step pyrolysis like method involving glucose and urea in a Teflon-lined autoclave. Palladium nanoparticles as were subsequently anchored onto the N-CQDs. This method enables the facile preparation of both nitrogen-doped and undoped carbon quantum dots. Palladium exhibited a hexagonal crystal structure, contributing to a larger surface area relative to its volume. We subsequently established a magneto-electrochemical system to investigate the influence of magnetic fields the electrocatalytic ORR in alkaline media. The experimental findings demonstrate that the magnetic field had a little effect on the kinetic region, whereas the mass transport region was significantly influenced, resulting in a 100 % increase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs, N-CQDs, r-CQDs, N-rCQDs, Pd/N-rCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized using a simple one-step pyrolysis like method. A mixture of glucose and urea was placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 190 °C for 7 h. The resulting product powder was dissolved in water, sonicated, and centrifuged to remove larger particles. The remaining supernatant was filtered using 0.22 μm filters. Finally, the mixture was dried to obtain the brown N-CQDs powder. To synthesize undoped CQDs, the same procedure was followed, but without the addition of urea. The r-CQDs and N-rCQDs have been refluxed by adding NaBH4 to bare CQDs and N-CQDs for 3h in the presence of N2 atmosphere. The structure of CQDs features several oxygen functional groups, including carboxylic acid (-COOH), aldehyde (HCO), and alcohols (-OH) [42]. Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) has been recognized for many years as a potent reducing agent, notably capable of converting carboxylic acids to primary alcohols [43]. Consequently, adding NaBH4 to the system achieves not only the reduction of metal ions to their metallic form but also the transformation of carboxylic acid functional groups presents on the CQDs into alcohol groups(r-CQDs).
To synthesize Pd/N-rCQDs, the following procedure was used:

First, 1 mL of PdCl2 (1 M) was added to 100 mg of N-CQDs in 50 mL of water and stirred for 1 h at 50 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere. Second, 3 g of NaBH4 was dissolved in water and added to the solution over 30 min. The temperature was then increased to 80 °C, and the mixture was refluxed for 12 h. Finally, the product (Pd/N-rCQDs) was centrifuged, washed with water several times, and dried at 80 °C.

A potentiostat-galvanostat (VSP-300) equipped with a three-electrode compartment system was used for electrochemical experiments. A platinum counter electrode, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode (KCl 1 M), and a glassy carbon working electrode (geometric area: 0.042 cm2) modified with synthesized catalysts were employed. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed in both N2- and O2-saturated alkaline electrolytes within a potential range of 0.2–1.5 V versus the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). A circulating water bath maintained a stable cell temperature by regulating the flow of heated or cooled water through the cell jacket. ORR activity was evaluated in a 0.1 M KOH solution saturated with oxygen. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements were conducted at a scan rate of 5 mV/s. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed at 0.6 V vs. RHE and a frequency range of 100 kHz to 100 mHz. A systematic investigation of magnetic field influence on the working electrode was conducted using U shape flat magnets with field strengths ranging from 0.2 to 0.35 T. A static magnetic field was applied to the working electrode using a U-shaped permanent magnet. The magnet was positioned such that its north (N) and south (S) poles were aligned along the plane of the working electrode surface, creating a perpendicular magnetic field oriented on it. The working electrode was placed between the poles to be exposed to this field. The counter electrode was located at a distance further away to minimize any direct magnetic field influence. Optimal performance was observed at 0.3 T, a value almost close with the magnetic field strength reported by Yang et al. [44].

Fig. 1 a(i and ii) shows the SEM image of Pd/N-rCQDs. The carbon quantum dots appear as small, bright dots on a dark background. The size and brightness of the dots can vary depending on the size and composition of the carbon quantum dots. Based on the information in the image, it is not possible to say definitively how large the CQDs or Pd are. Hence, in order further investigation, the TEM technique has been used. Fig. 1a(iii) demonstrates the TEM image of Pd/N-rCQDs. This figure shows a regular hexahedron well and represents our successful polyhedral synthesis with average Pd crystallite around 10 nm as illustrated in inset histogram. Its faceted form is consistent with the reported articles [45]. Fig. 1b(i) shows element mapping alongside the SEM imaging. It provides information on the distribution of the C, N, O, and Pd elements across the sample. It shows that Pd is uniformly distributed across the carbon dots. Fig. 1b(ii) shows the EDX of the Pd/N-rCQDs catalyst. The spectrum shows peaks for all four of the elements that were expected to be in the catalyst (C, N, O, and Pd). The presence of these peaks confirms that the catalyst does indeed contain these elements.

Fig. 1c shows the in-situ Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis of the CQDs, N-CQDs. Fig. 1c displays FTIR of N-CQDs, N-rCQDs, and Pd/N-rCQDs in the range of 4000−400 cm−1. In Fig. 1c(i), CQDs display the characteristic absorption bands at 3100–3450 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations in hydroxyl (OH) groups (associated with carboxylic acids and water) and carbonyl (CO) groups in carboxylic acids, respectively [46,47]. Additionally, peaks at 2990, 1320, and 1015 cm−1 indicate stretching of CH alkanes, deformation vibrations of carboxyl CC bonds, and C–O–C bonds, respectively. In comparison to CQDs, the intensities of rCQD absorption peaks obviously decrease, which is due to the significant reduction of oxygen-containing groups. The absorption peaks at 3100–3450 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1, which are further weakened, suggesting the removal of most functional groups [48,49]. From the other side, the intensity of the peak at 1320 cm−1, which corresponds to C–O bending vibrations, increases [50]. This suggests that CO groups are converted to C–O bonds during the reduction process. Fig. 1c(ii) shows that the characteristic peaks for N-CQDs are similar to those of CQDs, but with a significant decrease in intensity, demonstrating some carbonyl (CO) groups in N-CQDs are replaced by nitrogen atoms [46,48].

In this regard, the spectra of the N-CQDs absorption peak can be explained by the characteristic stretching and bending vibrations of amines and amides such as C–N and N–H bonds. The broad peak around 3100-3400 cm−1 comes from amino groups (N–H) as well as other functional groups containing oxygen and hydrogen (OH and COOH). Further, the peaks at 2850, 1685, 1325, and 1040 cm−1 correspond to the C–H, CN, C–N, and N–H, simultaneously indicating of the CO and C–O groups [51]. Finding amino groups, cyan groups, and both double (CN) and single (C–N) carbon-nitrogen bonds in the CQDs suggests nitrogen atoms have been incorporated into the material through a hydrothermal process. FTIR of N-rCQDs shows some absorption peaks that are consistent with those typically found in N-CQDs. Apparently, the peak associated with CC bonds (at 1325 cm−1) became stronger, suggesting that some carbonyl groups (CO) in the N-CQDs were reduced to simpler CC bonds. By analyzing the FTIR of Pd/N-rCQDs, a decrease in the intensity of peaks associated with oxygen-containing groups is observed. This suggests that the oxygen groups in the N-rCQDs might be coordinating or bonding with the palladium (Pd) centers [48].

Raman spectroscopy was employed to understand the structural characteristics of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and their derivative defect sites, either as doping or as support. This technique is valuable because graphitic materials typically exhibit distinct peaks corresponding to the ordered (G-band) and disordered (D-band) regions within their structure. The relative intensity of these peaks, expressed as the ID/IG ratio, serves as a quantitative measure of the concentration of defects within the carbon material. A high ID/IG ratio indicates the amorphous nature, and a high degree of graphitization gives a relatively lower ID/IG ratio. Fig. 2 a presents the Raman spectra of CQDs and N-CQDs, while Fig. 2b displays the Raman spectra of N-rCQDs and Pd/N-rCQDs nanostructures. The Raman spectra of all samples, including CQDs, N-CQDs, N-rCQDs, and Pd/N-rCQDs exhibit two characteristic peaks at around 1362, 1378, 1388, and 1380 cm−1, which correspond to D band, and 1570, 1578, 1544, and 1551 cm−1, which relate to the G bands, respectively. These peaks have an intensity ratio (ID/IG) of 0.722 (CQDs), 0.954 (N-CQDs), 1.036 (N-rCQDs), and 1.07 (Pd/N-rCQDs). The Pd/N-rCQDs exhibit teeth peaks at 626 cm−1 [52], which match with the Pd nanoparticles, suggesting their presence within the composite material. Furthermore, the progressive increase in the ID/IG ratio from CQDs (0.722) to N-rCQDs (1.036) strongly suggests a corresponding increase in the concentration of structural defects, particularly those disrupting the graphitic sp2 network, often referred to as across-plane defects or edge defects [53]. This interpretation is further supported by the observed broadening of both the D and G bands in the spectra of the nitrogen-doped and reduced carbon quantum dots (N-rCQDs and Pd/N-rCQDs), which is characteristic of a decrease in crystalline domain size and an increase in structural disorder [53].

Fig. 2c shows X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) for a material fabricated with palladium nanoparticles (Pd) based on N-CQDs, obtained after synthesis and after 2000 cycles. The pattern reveals a broad peak at 16.90° and 43.37° corresponding to the graphitic component of N-CQDs support, specifically the (002) and (101) planes. Additionally, there are broader peaks at 39.77°, 46.02°, 67.58°, and 81.43°, which are attributes to the (111), (200), (220) and (311) planes of the face-centered cubic structure of Pd, respectively (JCPDS no. 46–1043) [54]. To demonstrate the catalyst's stability, we conducted XRD analysis of the Pd/N-rCQDs material after 2000 CV cycles and compared it to the pre-reaction XRD. The post-cycling XRD pattern showed no substantial alterations in the material's structure, confirming its structural stability.

The XPS survey spectrum of Pd/N-rCQDs, shown in Fig. 2d, reveals characteristic peaks corresponding to C 1s (284 eV), N 1s (399 eV), O 1s (531.0 eV), and Pd 3d5/2 (334 eV) core levels [55]. High-resolution Pd 3d XPS analysis (Fig. 2e) further revealed the presence of both metallic Pd0 (335 and 339.5 eV) and oxidized Pd 2+ (336.4 and 340.8 eV) species, with the latter attributed to surface oxidation upon air exposure [54,55].

Similarly, the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2f) displays four distinct peaks at 395.2 eV, 397.02 eV, 399.83 eV, and 404 eV, which are related to pyridine, pyrrole, quaternary N, and oxidized N, respectively [56]. Fig. 2g shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s. There are five peaks observed at 286.85, 285.28, 283.68, 282.36, 280.78 eV, which correspond to the CO/CN, C–O/C–N, C–C/C–H, CC, and aromatic carbon [55,57,58], respectively.

The nitrogen content in the catalysts was quantified using CHN elemental analysis (EA). The results, presented in Table 1 , indicate a decrease in nitrogen content from 20.38 to 8.2 wt% upon the deposition of metal catalysts. The surface elemental composition and chemical bonding states were analyzed using XPS [59], enabling a comparative analysis of bulk (EA) and surface (XPS) elemental compositions, as summarized in Table 1.

To evaluate the electrocatalytic properties of N-rCQDs, cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were conducted in both N2- and O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solutions at a scan rate of 50 mV/s (Fig. 3 a). In both N2- and O2-saturated, the CV curves exhibited a pseudocapacitive behavior characteristic of nitrogen-doped carbons within the potential range of 1.4 to 0.8 V. However, in the absence of oxygen at the potential between 0.8 and 0.15 V, the CV spectra revealed the removal of various oxygen-containing functional groups from the carbon surface through electrochemical reduction [60]. In contrast, when the electrolyte was saturated with oxygen, N-rCQDs exhibited a significant reduction current between 0.8 V and 0.2 V, with a prominent reduction peak at 0.4 V and onset potential at 0.82 V. Additionally, Fig. 3b shows the CV curves of Pd/N-rCQDs in the presence and absence of O2. Compared to pure N-rCQDs, Pd/N-rCQDs demonstrated superior ORR performance, characterized by more positive peak potentials (Epeak), higher peak currents, and higher onset potentials (0.99 V) and half wave potential (E1/2 = 0.82V). These enhancements can be attributed to the synergistic effects of nitrogen doping and palladium deposition. Furthermore, comparative analysis with previously published data reveals that Pd/N-rCQDs demonstrate superior onset potential and limiting current density compared to platinum-based CQDs (Eonset = 0.925 V) [61].

The electroreduction of oxygen molecules can be influenced by an external magnetic field, with the effect varying based on the field's direction and intensity. Fig. 3c illustrates the ORR polarization curves for Pd/N-rCQDs catalysts in a 0.1 M KOH solution at a glassy carbon electrode (GC) and a rotation speed of 1600 rpm, measured at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. The CV curves demonstrate the impact of the external magnetic field on the ORR process, as evidenced by a little positive shift in the onset potential (1.01V). Electrochemical analysis reveals that the mixed kinetic-diffusional region exhibits a greater sensitivity to external factors than the purely kinetic region, as evidenced by an 80 mV shift in the half-wave potential (E1/2). Notably, the diffusion-limited region demonstrates a significant susceptibility to magnetic fields, resulting in current enhancements exceeding 100 % (e.g., the current density at 0.6 V changes from −5 mA/cm2 to −10 mA/cm2 in the presence of a magnetic field). The observed results align precisely with a recent report authored by Vensaus et al. [32].

Moreover, to compare our catalysts with a benchmark, we compared the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) of the Pd/N-rCQDs in the presence and absence of a magnetic field with commercial standard Pt/C. As shown in Fig. S1e, the Eonset and E1/2 of Pt/C are 0.95 V and 0.83 V, respectively, which are lower than those of even non-magnetic Pd/N-rCQDs.

To assess the magnetic field's influence on the ORR, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed with (200 mT–350 mT) and without an applied magnetic field, over a frequency range of 100 kHz to 100 mHz at a potential of 0.6 V vs. RHE. The resulting Nyquist plots exhibited two characteristic features: a high-frequency semicircle attributed to the charge-transfer process at the electrode-electrolyte interface, and a low-frequency feature associated with oxygen transport within the solution [1]. These data were analyzed using a simplified equivalent circuit to determine the solution resistance (R1), charge transfer resistance (R2 or Rct), and mass transfer resistance (R3 or Rmt) and constant phase elements(CPE1 and 2). Nyquist plots under static conditions showed that non-magnetized Pd/N-rCQDs exhibited larger diameters for both high-frequency (Rct = 25 Ωcm2) and low-frequency (Rmt = 85 Ωcm2) features compared to magnetized catalysts. Specifically, with increasing magnetic field strength (200 mT: Rct = 20 Ωcm2, Rmt = 65.5 Ω cm2; 250 mT: Rct = 18Ωcm2, Rmt = 56Ωcm2; 300 mT: Rct = 14.5Ωcm2, Rmt = 33.5Ωcm2; 350 mT: Rct = 14.3 Ω cm2, Rmt = 33.5Ωcm2).This suggests that the application of a magnetic field resulted in a modest enhancement of electron transfer kinetics and a more significant improvement in oxygen transport within the solution towards the catalyst layer, potentially due to the ferromagnetic-like configuration (spin-charge distribution) and Lorentz force.

To assess the electrocatalytic activity of magnetized and non-magnetized Pd/N-rCQDs, LSV by using rotating disk electrode (RDE) tests were conducted at various rotation speeds in an alkaline medium. Fig. 3e and g displays curves for these catalysts. As the rotation rate increases, the current density also increases significantly. Notably, the onset potential of magnetized Pd/N-rCQDs is more positive compared to non-magnetized Pd/N-rCQDs. Fig. 3f and h shows the Koutecky-Levich (K-L) plots derived from the LSV curves. The electron transfer number (n) can be calculated from these plots. The results indicate that the n value for magnetized Pd/N-rCQDs is 4.2, while for non-magnetized Pd/N-rCQDs it is 3.6. This suggests that both catalysts primarily follow a 4-electron pathway for ORR. The trend of the number of transferred electrons is given in (Fig.s1a-d). Furthermore, LSV of Pd/N-rCQDs (Fig. 3i) revealed a change in the Tafel slope from 40 mV/dec in the absence of a magnetic field to 36 mV/dec under a 300 mT field. The application of an external magnetic field influences the surface properties of the paramagnetic Pd nanoparticles (NPs), inducing a transition towards a ferromagnetic spin configuration [40]. This altered spin state could affect the adsorption energies and kinetics of crucial ORR intermediates (e.g., superoxide and perhydroxyl species) [62], potentially leading to shift in the rate-determining step or a change in the relative surface coverage of these species.

Furthermore, we examined the impact of the magnetic field on the performance of N-rCQDs. By comparing the electrochemical behavior in the kinetic and concentration polarization regions as depicted in Fig. S1f, we observed that only the concentration-limited region was significantly influenced by the magnetic field. This selective effect on mass transport is consistent with the expected influence of the Lorentz force.

To assess the viability of these catalysts for alkaline direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC) applications, their resistance to methanol crossover was evaluated via chronoamperometry. Fig. 3j presents the current-time (i-t) profiles of Pt/C and Pd/N-rCQDs electrodes following the addition of 0.2 M methanol. Notably, the Pd/N-rCQDs electrode exhibited negligible variation in its oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) current after methanol addition at 900s, demonstrating exceptional methanol tolerance and high selectivity against methanol oxidation. Conversely, the Pt/C electrode displayed an immediate current jump, indicative of the onset of the methanol oxidation reaction. These different chronoamperometric responses emphasize the significantly enhanced methanol tolerance of Pd/N-rCQDs compared to commercial Pt/C as an ORR catalyst, highlighting its potential for improved alkaline DMFC performance.

A mixture of glucose and urea was subjected to a simple, low-temperature pyrolysis process to synthesize N-CQDs with a high nitrogen content of 20 %. Subsequently, palladium nanoparticles were anchored onto the N-CQDs.

Various characterization techniques, including FTIR, Raman, CHN elemental analysis, XRD, XPS and TEM, were employed to investigate the functional groups, defects, bulk and surface composition, and microstructure of the synthesized materials.

In an alkaline solution, Pd/N-rCQDs exhibited significantly enhanced electrocatalytic activity for the ORR. The combination of nitrogen doping and palladium deposition contributed to an increase in the number of active sites and improved the ORR process, as evidenced by cyclic voltammetry (CV), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). To quantify the effects of a magnetic field, an external magnetic field was applied to the working electrode during the ORR measurements. Our findings indicate that the magnetic field has little effect on the kinetic region. However, in diffusion-limited reactions, such as the ORR, the magnetic field can lead to substantial improvements exceeding 100 %.

Karim Kakaei: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yasemin Aykut: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ayşe Bayrakçeken: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Mehdi Mahdavi: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported by the Scientific and Technological Research Council of Türkiye (TÜBİTAK 2221 program) (project No. 1059B212400282).
